coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots toward highly sensitive detection of formaldehyde vapors using QCM sensor

Abdelghafar, Mona E.
Torad, Nagy L.
Yamauchi, Yusuke

coredata.dc:description: In the present study, an eco-friendly method for the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is demonstrated using hydrothermal treatment of laurel leaves. The optical and structural characteristics of the prepared CQDs are investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron (XPS), fluorescent and UV–visible spectroscopies, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) sensor designed and modified with CQDs is capable of detecting formaldehyde vapors in the presence of other interfering chemical-vapor analytes. The changes in the frequency of the QCM sensor are linearly correlated with the injected formaldehyde concentrations. The sensing properties of formaldehyde, including sensitivity and reversibility, are investigated. Detection of formaldehyde in the presence of humidity is carefully discussed for home or workplace room environment use. The adsorption kinetics of various VOCs vapors are also calculated and discussed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, an eco-friendly method for the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is demonstrated using hydrothermal treatment of laurel leaves. The optical and structural characteristics of the prepared CQDs are investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron (XPS), fluorescent and UV–visible spectroscopies, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) sensor designed and modified with CQDs is capable of detecting formaldehyde vapors in the presence of other interfering chemical-vapor analytes. The changes in the frequency of the QCM sensor are linearly correlated with the injected formaldehyde concentrations. The sensing properties of formaldehyde, including sensitivity and reversibility, are investigated. Detection of formaldehyde in the presence of humidity is carefully discussed for home or workplace room environment use. The adsorption kinetics of various VOCs vapors are also calculated and discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, the growing evolution of economic activity has a negative impact on environment protection, which threatens human life throughout the world due to the presence of a wide variety of pollutants (Ayad et al., 2018; Amer et al., 2018, 2019; Rad et al., 2021; Doustkhah et al., 2021; Alidagi et al., 2019; Tümay, 2021; Duran et al., 2014). Of these pollutants, environmental hazardous volatile organic compounds (VOCs), such as aliphatic and aromatic hydrocarbons that became one of the major concerns (Von Oettingen, 1942). Among these deleterious substances, formaldehyde, a colorless and strong-smelling gas, is utilized in the manufacture of many products such as construction materials, household products, glues, and adhesives. Formaldehyde can also be used as food preservative and to preserve products, such as antiseptics, cosmetics, and medicines (Xu et al., 2007). However, the exposure to its vapor, at levels higher than 0.1 parts per million (ppm), might cause health disorders such as burning sensations of eyes, throat, and nose, along with coughing, skin irritation, wheezing, and nausea (McLaughlin, 1994).

To avoid or reduce the dangerous effect on human life, many techniques were developed to detect formaldehyde vapors. Among these techniques are the use of fluorescence-based system using standard microscope glass slide as a transducer (Descamps et al., 2012), Cd-doped TiO2–SnO2 sensor (Zeng et al., 2009), a fluorescent probe based on 2,5-dihydroxy-p-benzenedicarbonamide (Cao et al., 2020) and functionalized chitosan (Li et al., 2018), polydimethylsiloxane microfluidic chip (Weng et al., 2009), and nickel doped P nanozeolite carbon paste electrode (Azizi et al., 2016) were reported. Although these analytical techniques have high sensitivity and selectivity, they are cost ineffective as they require expensive organics, several steps for operation, and are time-consuming. Hence, the aim of the present work is to use the quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) (Ayad et al., 2009, 2012; Ayad and Torad, 2009) technique in the detection of trace concentrations of chemical analytes, not only because of its sensitivity and accuracy but also the ease of in situ operation (Ponrathnam et al., 2015).

A sensitive functional materials nanoarchitectonic coated the Au surface of the QCM electrode can be utilized as sensitive layers toward the design and performance evaluations of the chemical QCM sensors to detect particular chemical analytes (Si et al., 2007). Sensitive materials nanoarchitectonics, including organic, inorganic, and their composites have been investigated (Torad et al., 2019). The utilization of conducting polymers such as polypyrrole and polyaniline, and mesoporous silica films as sensitive coating materials on the Au electrode of the QCM for the detection of pH, acids, chlorinated hydrocarbons, alcohols, and aliphatic amines vapor has been introduced by the current research team (Ayad et al., 2008a, 2008b, 2014; Su and Chuang, 2017; Cimpoca et al., 2010). On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) of a diameter smaller than 10 nm have attracted attention of researchers, being a sensitive layer coated on the QCM electrode to fabricate chemical sensors (Kan et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2019). Because of their high chemical stability, biocompatibility, water solubility, fluorescence properties, low toxicity, easy surface functionalization, and easy preparation, CQDs are known to be less hazardous compared to other traditional metal-based semiconductor quantum dots, including CdS, CdSe, and PbSe, which have many disadvantages due to their toxicity, high cost, and sophisticated synthesis procedures (Zhi et al., 2019; Hsu et al., 2012; Li et al., 2012; Pramanik et al., 2018). Their electronic, mechanical, optical, and chemical properties are attractive in various applications including photocatalysis (Hu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2016), optoelectronics (Litvin et al., 2017; Li et al., 2015), biomedicine (William et al., 2006), catalysis (Xu et al., 2018), bioimaging (Zhu et al., 2011), tissue engineering (Smith et al., 2006), electronic devices (Ravindran et al., 2003), metals detection (Ansi and Renuka, 2020), and sensing (Wang et al., 2021).

The amorphous and crystalline cores of CQDs have substantially graphitic carbon (sp 2-carbon) fused by diamond-like sp 3-hybridized carbon insertions. This justifies the capabilities of CQDs in electron transfer in addition its adsorption properties (Roy et al., 2016). Their nanoscale dimensions present a high aspect ratio that provides multiple reactive sites available for gas targeting that enhances their performances in gas sensor applications (Shen et al., 2012). Based on these properties, CQDs have been used as sensitive substrate coating the QCM electrode used as sensors to detect environmental pollutants, VOCs, explosives, and humidity. Okur and co-workers reported the detection of humidity using CdS quantum dots (Demir et al., 2011). Wang et al. (Li et al., 2019) recently used PbS quantum dots to detect NO2 at high sensitivity and selectivity. The same group, also, developed a novel sensor based on graphene quantum dots-chitosan composite coating the QCM electrode, demonstrating high sensitivity, excellent reproducibility, long-term stability for humidity, and short response/recovery time (Qi et al., 2019).

Carbon quantum dots are synthesized from different material sources with different techniques. Many protocols were reported for the synthesis of CQDs, such as ultrasonic passivation technique, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, arc discharge, microwave mediated synthesis, and thermal cracking of organic compounds (Wang and Hu, 2014). These methods suffer from challenging drawbacks, such as the involvement of sophisticated instruments. Recently, the green synthesis approaches attracted the attention for preparing CQDs considering both the material sources and the methodology. For example, the preparation of CQDs using hydrothermal carbonization, at specific conditions, represents a green approach (Algarra et al., 2014; Kaur et al., 2018). In the context of enhancing such approach, various natural carbon sources such as coriander leaves, grape juice, lime juice, milk, orange juice, grass, lignite coal, watermelon, green tea, potatoes, egg, and lotus root, have been used to prepare CQDs. These natural carbon sources are available, cheap, and non-toxic (Das et al., 2018).

The current study aims at the synthesis of CQDs via a facile method by employing the hydrothermal treatment of a natural carbon source of laurel leaves. These leaves (Laurus nobilis) originate from a flowering plant and a source of several popular herbs and spices used for a wide variety of foods such as meat, soup, sauces, confectionery, and fish, particularly among Mediterranean region. Additionally, the essential oil of laurel is widely used in the perfume, soap, and drugs industries (Chahal et al., 2017). Laurel has been reported to have an antiulcer and antidiabetic effect. Furthermore, it is considered to be a good source of minerals (Ca, K, Fe, Mg, Mn, Zn, Cu). The second aim is to use a thin film of the prepared CQDs, coated onto the QCM electrode, as a sensor for the detection of formaldehyde vapors in air. The fabricated sensor based on CQDs is simple, sensitive, rapid, and reproducible. The third aim is to investigate the kinetics of vapors adsorption onto the CQDs film coated on the QCM electrode. The last aim of this study is to discuss the effect of humidity on the detection of formaldehyde.

Pyridine (99%), pyrrole (99%), and aniline (99%) were purchased from (LOBA Chemie, India). Formaldehyde (40%), potassium hydroxide (solid, 99.5%), potassium carbonate (solid, 99.5%) sodium chloride (solid, 98%) and potassium dichromate (solid, 99.8%) (ADWIC, EGYPT) were used as received. Laurel leaves were obtained from a local supermarket in Tanta, Egypt.

Herein, CQDs were prepared from laurel leaves. These leaves were thoroughly washed in water, then ethanol to remove any impurities and then dried in air for 24 h. Afterward, the leaves were ground into a fine powder from which 5 g were taken and mixed with distilled water. The mixture was then introduced into a 100 mL-sized Teflon–lined autoclave and thermally heated at 150 °C for 4 h. The solution showed a golden yellow color, which confirms the formation of CQDs and complete carbonization (Lu et al., 2013; Sabet and Mahdavi, 2019; Kumar et al., 2014). The sample was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to remove any undesired impurities and dialyzed using 3.5 kDa dialysis bags for further purification (Sabet and Mahdavi, 2019; Yadav et al., 2018). The yield of the synthesized laurel CQDs was estimated as 20%. A schematic illustration of the preparation procedures of CQDs from laurel leaves through the hydrothermal approach is shown in Fig. 1 .

JEOLJEM-2100F was used to study the bulk morphology of the obtained CQDs using the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by drop-casting a proper dilution of CQDs onto the carbon-coated copper grids. A GNR APD–2000 PRO diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA) was used to measure the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CQDs in the step scan mode. The FTIR spectra of CQDs were also measured via Perkin–Elmer Spectrum BX spectrometer using KBr pellets as a reference in the region of 400–4000 cm−1. The fluorescence measurements were applied with Hitachi F-2700 using Xenon lamp light source of 150 W. A fluorescence lifetime measuring system (Horiba FluoroCube 3000U-EYM1) of excitation wavelength 372 nm was used to record the fluorescence decay. UV Lamp (6 W, at 365 nm, 0.08 A) (Model-Upland, CA, USA) was used for irradiation. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS using Multilab2000, Thermo Fisher Scientiﬁc, USA) was employed to identify the elemental composition of the synthesized CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots-based QCM gas sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
GW frequency counter (GFC-8055G model) was used to measure the crystal resonance frequency. An AT 5 MHz quartz crystal with gold electrodes was used. The gold coated QCM electrode was washed several times with distilled water, acetone, and ethanol, and finally the crystal was left to dry, and the QCM resonance frequency was then recorded. The gold electrode was then coated with CQDs via the drop-coating method (Ayad et al., 2014). After coating, the QCM crystal was kept in an oven at 40 °C for 20 min to ensure complete drying of the coated film. The frequency shift resulting from the deposition of the film was calculated by subtracting the resonance frequency before and after the film application. The measurements were done in a polyethylene (PE) cell with 120 mL internal volume. A small fan was fixed inside the cell to ensure complete evaporation and homogenous distribution of the injected analyte in the cell. The desired analyte was then injected with a Hamilton microsyringe into the other side of the cell through a rubber stopper (injection septum). The concentration of injected VOCs (C) in the cell is determined using the following equation:
(1)
C =
ρ V
l
V
where, C is the concentration of the injected analyte (mg L−1), ρ is the density of the analyte in mg L−1, V l and V represent the liquid volume and working cell volume in L, respectively (Ayad et al., 2019).

After saturation of the QCM electrode with the injected analyte and the stability of the crystal resonance frequency, the CQDs-coated QCM was kept in the oven at 40 °C for 20 min to ensure complete desorption of the analyte. The back shift of the crystal frequency to its initial value confirms the good reversibility of the proposed sensor. The measurements were done at the ambient temperature (∼24 ± 1 °C).

The response and the frequency change (Δf) of the sensor are given by the equation: Δf = f air - f gas, where f air and f gas are the measured frequencies of air and desired gases, respectively. The sensitivity of the gas sensor is expressed by the relationship between slope coefficient of response to gas concentrations curve: S = Δf/(Δ Conc .), where Δ Conc. is the gas range of linear concentration.

The humidity effect onto the CQDs film and the CQDs-based formaldehyde sensor was examined through subjecting the CQDs film-coated QCM to saturated solutions of different salts in the absence and presence of formaldehyde vapor (2.9 mg L−1), respectively. The experimental setup for studying the effect of humidity on the detection of formaldehyde is depicted in Fig. 2 . A PE bottle (120 mL capacity) containing saturated solutions of KOH, K2CO3, NaCl, and K2Cr2O7 at 25 °C were employed to provide 9%, 43%, 75%, and 98% relative humidity (RH) levels, respectively. Various RH levels were exposed to the QCM-based sensor. By switching the flask, the measurement was completed in a static exposure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In the current work, laurel leaves are used as a green and natural source for the preparation of CQDs using the hydrothermal technique to produce a light brown aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 1. This indicates the successful carbonization of the laurel leaves.

Fig. 3 depicts the different characterizations of the synthesized laurel derived CQDs. Fig. 3(a) shows the TEM image of the CQDs that reveals their uniform distribution and monodispersity. In addition, the diameter of CQDs is about 4.5 nm shown in Fig. 3(b). The high-resolution TEM image could not be observed at high magnification, and it deos not show lattice structures of CQDs, indicating that the CQDs are amorphous dots. Several scientists have revealed the presence of an sp 2 crystal carbon, and most of the CQDs have weak crystallinity (Chaudhary et al., 2020). Similar to natural source-derived CQDs, lemon juice-derived CQDs are found to have an amorphous nature (Tadesse et al., 2020).

Fig. 3 (c) displays the UV–visible spectra of the as-prepared CQDs displaying two peaks, one at about 275 nm and a small broadness at about 322 nm that are assigned to n-σ* transition of CO and π-π* transition of CC band, respectively, which are characteristic to the CQDs (Lu et al., 2013). These CQDs are found to be stable for about two months, which can be attributed to the electrostatic repulsions between different functional groups present on its surface. The digital image of the CQDs aqueous sample is shown in Fig. 3(c), displaying its light brown color under visible light. However, a strong green luminescence appears under UV light (Zheng et al., 2018).

The bandgap (E
g ) of the synthesized CQDs was calculated and found to be 5.71 eV according to the Tauc relation (Fig. 3(d)) by plotting (αhν)n versus energy (eV). To calculate E g, a tangent line was drawn on the curve where α = 0 (Li et al., 2013). Then,
(2)
(
α h ν
)
n
= K
(
h ν
–
E g
)
where, “α” is absorption coefficient, “K” is energy independent constant, “hν” is the incident photon energy, and E
g is the optical band gap (Sabet and Mahdavi, 2019).

To explore the fluorescence emission of the CQDs, a fluorescence study was performed using different excitation wavelengths in the range 300–500 nm as shown in Fig. 3(e). The emission spectra are shifted from 430 nm to 530 nm, with an apparent fluorescence intensity decrease with broadening upon increasing the excitation wavelength exhibiting a second less-intense band. This is consistent with the results of previous work (Ghosh et al., 2014). This red shift may be attributed to the disparate emissive sites present at the CQDs surface, which are responsible for the fluorescence emission (Gu et al., 2017). The luminescence decay curve of laurel CQDs is shown in Fig. 3(f). The lifetime data are fitted well to a triple exponential function. The received two lifetimes of 0.45 and 3.7 ns are attributed to the intrinsic state and the surface state, respectively.

The FTIR measurements, Fig. 4 (a), are used to explore the surface functional groups and the chemical structure of the laurel CQDs. A broad band that combines 3462 and 3280 cm−1 indicates the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations (Wang et al., 2019; Bano et al., 2018) which enhance the stability and solubility of the synthesized CQDs. The stretching vibrations of C–H bonds appear at 2935 and 2850 cm−1. The broad peak around 1637 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration of carbon backbone. The presence of two bands at 1124 and 1055 cm−1 confirms the stretching vibration of C–N and C–O peaks, respectively (Murugan et al., 2019). Such results indicate that CQDs are functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic, and amino groups, derived from organic moieties in laurel leaves, which reinforce CQDs solubility (Wang et al., 2019). The XRD analysis of the synthesized CQDs shows a broad peak at 2θ = 24.1 (belongs to hexagonal carbon), which confirms the amorphous carbon nature of CQDs (Fig. 4(b)) (Zheng et al., 2018).

Wide-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 4(c)) displays three main peaks at 285.45, 400, and 532.2 eV that are related to the states of C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. This observation proves the main existence of C, N, and O elements in the laurel CQDs. The shifts in binding energy were calibrated using C1s at 285.0 eV. The plot of high-resolution XPS C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra is shown in Fig. 4(d–f). The enlarged C1s spectrum of CQDs derived from Laurel (Fig. 4(d)) contains five deconvoluted peaks. The graphitic sp2-carbon structure (CC) and the amorphous sp 3-carbon (C–C) are the origin of C1 and C2 peaks, respectively. The other three peaks, C3, C4, and C5, are characteristic to the direct attachment of carbon to oxygen (C–OH), nitrogen (C–N), and carbonyl (CO) groups, respectively (Torad et al., 2014). The N1s spectra of laurel CQDs, Fig. 4(e), are deconvoluted into three peaks. A low content of C–N was observed in the spectrum of N1s. The pyridinic-N and the pyrrolic-N are responsible for N1 and N2 peaks, respectively (Torad et al., 2014). Additionally, the N3 peak accounts for the quaternary nitrogen, that is the graphitic-N incorporated into the graphitic carbon framework of CQDs, and hence denotes the development of a graphitic-like carbon structure. Most of the doped N is present as a pair of π-electron with pyrrolic and pyridinic carbon rings as shown in Table 1 and Table 2 . The sp2-nitrogen (CN) may act as an electron donor that could facilitate the diffusion of guest molecules for chemisorption with organic vapors.

The structural defects of unsaturated carbon atoms at carbon edges arose form the existence of N atoms in the carbon framework, such that when exposed to air. As a consequence, they possess the ability to react with physically absorbed oxygen to constitute oxygen functional groups (Torad et al., 2014). The deconvoluted high-resolution spectrum of O1s is shown in Fig. 4(f). Accordingly, the physically absorbed oxygen accounts for O1 peak. Further, O2 and O3 peaks are attributed to the carbonyl (−CO) and hydroxyl C–OH/C–O–C surface groups, respectively (Lu et al., 2012; Murugan and Sundramoorthy, 2018; Yu et al., 2017). Actually, there exists a major content of oxygen in the laurel CQDs that arise from the carbon-oxygen bonds in the deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. 4(d)). In spite of the basic nature of the oxygen functional groups, the laurel CQDs show an excellent sensing material toward polar vapor analytes through green synthesis and simple carbonization of Laurel. Moreover, the trace minerals found in Laurel leaves interpret the presence of small traces of Na (Fig. 4(c)) (Lu et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots coated QCM detection VOCs
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of sp 2 and sp 3-hybridized carbon in addition to the presence of surface functional groups grants the CQDs the ability to adsorb VOCs depending on the chemical structure of the analytes. Furthermore, the laurel CQDs have the merit of high surface area, and hence their application as a gas sensor is highly recommended. Based on these properties, laurel CQDs are coated on the QCM electrode as a sensor for the detection of environmental pollutants and VOCs vapors, mainly formaldehyde with a high sensing accuracy and selectivity. In the following experiments, the frequency changes are normalized by the mass of the films in the active area of QCM to avoid any changes resulting from using CQDs films with different thicknesses (Severin and Lewis, 2000).

The CQDs-modified QCM sensor was exposed to formaldehyde vapor of different concentrations in the range of 5.82–46.57 mg L−1. It is observed that the frequency underwent a dramatic decrease upon injection of formaldehyde concentration, implying the adsorption of formaldehyde vapor onto the laurel CQDs. Fig. 5 (a) shows the relationship between frequency changes (Δf) against time at different formaldehyde concentrations. Accordingly, Δf was found to increase linearly with time till the steady-state. Following the equilibrium, the QCM frequency was back shifted to its initial frequency after placing the CQDs-modified QCM electrode saturated with chemical-analyte molecules in a convection oven at 40 °C for 20 min. Getting the initial frequency of the QCM electrode indicates the full desorption of the vapor analyte. Besides, the more the formaldehyde vapor concentration, the more the magnitude of Δf of the QCM. A linear plot of Δf against the formaldehyde vapor concentrations is obtained, achieving high sensitivity of 41.0 Hz mg−1 L (R = 0.968) as shown in Fig. 5(b).

To check the sensitivity of the sensor toward formaldehyde, the resultant CQDs-modified QCM sensor is also utilized to detect other interfering chemical-vapor analytes, including aniline, pyridine, and pyrrole vapors. Fig. 5(c–h) expresses the exposure of laurel-derived CQDs coating on the QCM electrode to aniline, pyridine, and pyrrole vapors. Upon injection of aniline into the working cell, an obvious decrease is observed in the quartz crystal frequency as shown in Fig. 5(c). As clearly seen, the crystal frequency increases linearly with increasing the concentration of injected aniline molecules ranging from 7.28 to 72.85 mg L−1 and a linear relationship between Δf and the aniline concentration is obtained, achieving a lower sensor sensitivity of 10.3 Hz mg−1 L (R 2 = 0.959) (Fig. 5(d)). Similarly, the fabricated CQDs-modified QCM sensor was also tested toward pyridine and pyrrole in an injected concentrations range of about 6.0–55 mg L−1, demonstrating a gradual decrease in the crystal frequency due to the adsorption uptake of analyte vapors on the surface of CQDs coated QCM electrode as shown in Fig. 5(e, g). Linear calibration curves as a function of pyridine and pyrrole concentration are plotted (Fig. 5(f, h)), achieving sensitivities of 10.66 and 7.23 Hz mg−1 L, respectively.

The sensitivities, standard deviations (SD), limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ) (Torad et al., 2021), and correlation coefficients (R 2) are tabulated in Table 3 . These results confirm the high sensing capability of CQDs toward formaldehyde vapor in air regardless of the other interfering chemical-vapor analytes.

This work establishes a sensor discrimination of vaporized formaldehyde by our synthesized CQDs material using the QCM sensor technique (Fig. 6 (a)). The selectivity of the CQDs-based sensor toward formaldehyde was demonstrated by exposing the CQDs-coated QCM electrode to other various chemical-vapor analytes of the same concentration (10 mg L−1) with the resulting Δfs being 1000, 336, 301, 282, 164, 157, 147, 134, 104, 85, and 62 Hz for formaldehyde, methylene chloride, carbon tetrachloride, ethanol, aniline, methanol, pyrrole, pyridine, xylene, benzene, and toluene, respectively (Fig. 6(b)). Accordingly, it can be observed that laurel-derived CQDs-modified QCM sensor displays an enormous drop of frequency during the first adsorption that is responsible for the large uptake of acidic molecules and the large number of active sites in the carbon film, leading to about 6.1, 6.8, 7.5, 9.6, 11.8, 16.1 times increase in response over aniline, pyrrole, pyridine, xylene, benzene, and toluene, respectively. On the other hand, CQDs-modified QCM sensor exhibits lower sensitivities toward vaporized aromatic hydrocarbons, such as benzene, toluene and xylene, giving only small frequency changes of 85 Hz, 62 Hz, and 104 Hz, respectively, indicating that these analytes favor physisorption rather than chemisorption. These results show the potential capability of laurel-derived CQDs material having surface functional groups for a high sensitivity in the detection and quantification of formaldehyde vapor in air over other interfering VOCs.

Table 4 compares the performance of the CQDs-coated QCM sensor with that of other materials reported in the literature toward formaldehyde detection. Further, the CQDs-modified QCM sensor displays superior sensing performance than the previously reported sensors based on various nanomaterials (Table 4).

For the sake of comparison and confirming the role of laurel-derived CQDs as a sensitive layer toward formaldehyde vapor, the detection of formaldehyde vapor using unmodified QCM electrode was examined as shown in Fig. 7 . The exposure of the unmodified QCM electrode to 10 mg L-1 of formaldehyde vapor shows only Δf value of 45 Hz. On the contrary, the CQDs-coated QCM electrode shows Δf value of 1000 Hz, which indicates the high essential role of CQDs as a coating film onto the QCM electrode.

To unveil the sensing mechanism of CQDs toward formaldehyde molecules, FTIR spectra were measured after exposing the synthesized laurel derived CQDs to formaldehyde molecules as shown in Fig. 8 . After exposure to formaldehyde vapor, there is a bathochromic shift of OH stretching peak from 3462 cm–1 to 3417 cm−1 with broadening (Fig. 8). Clearly, the N–H peak observed at 1380 cm−1 is very weak or almost disappeared in addition to the appearance of a new peak at 1202 cm−1 representing a C–N stretching vibration band that all indicate the chemisorption of formaldehyde molecules onto the surface of CQDs (Zong et al., 2018).

The good sensitivity and selectivity of CQDs to HCHO relative to other analytes can be attributed to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present in the CQDs sample. These acidic radicals offer energetic sites for the chemisorption of polar molecules, such as HCHO. Based on previous simulation studies (Manna et al., 2018; He et al., 2022; Carter et al., 2011), the adsorption of HCHO occurs via hydrogen bonding between the oxygen atom of carboxyl group and the hydrogen atom of HCHO. Additionally, the hydroxyl group in CQDs also forms hydrogen bonding with HCHO. The electron-withdrawing nature of CQDs enables better selectivity toward HCHO over other analytes. These results are encouraging and show the potential capability of the CQDs to retain selectivity and high sensing response for HCHO vapor in air regardless of the interfering compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3.4. Reversibility and reproducibility of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility is an important tool that evaluates the sensor efficacy. The CQDs-coated QCM sensor was exposed to 10 mg L−1 of formaldehyde vapor. After full formaldehyde adsorption, the reproducibility of CQDs was estimated in terms of base frequency (Fig. 9 ). After injection of formaldehyde, Δf of 389 Hz was obtained due to the adsorption uptake of formaldehyde vapors. The analyte vapor-saturated CQDs-modified QCM electrodes were added in a convection oven at 40 °C for 20 min for desorption of the analyte till getting the baseline response. The initial frequency indicates the complete formaldehyde vapor desorption. When the QCM electrode was connected through the working cell, the CQDs-coated QCM sensor was almost recovered to its initial frequency. As shown in Fig. 9, the cycling tests show a good reproducibility of the CQDs sample. As shown in Fig. 9, the injection of the same formaldehyde concentration provided almost the same Δf value (376 Hz). The sorbed formaldehyde molecules were rapidly desorbed at 40 °C, and the frequency returns to its initial value. The CQDs-based QCM sensor exhibit a perfect repeatability as a sensing material during alternate exposure and removal of formaldehyde, with about 4.37% decrease in frequency change. This behavior indicates that laurel CQDs can be applied for sensing various VOCs several times without any serious damage.

The humidity may result in a selectivity drift during the detection of VOCs. As a result, it should not be neglected and an interference test is required, especially if the QCM sensor is used in a workplace room environment. The effect of %RH on the sensing performance of CQDs toward formaldehyde vapor was measured by exposing CQDs-modified QCM sensor toward saturated solutions of different %RH (from 9 to 98%) in the absence of formaldehyde and measuring the QCM sensor frequency. As shown in Fig. 10 , the frequency responses of CQDs-coated QCM sensor increases with increasing %RH from 9% to 98% in the working cell. This shows that a high-water content (98 %RH) in the working cell led to a high sensor response, demonstrating the high sensing activity of CQDs-based QCM sensor toward humidity. Whereas, in the presence of formaldehyde, CQDs-based QCM sensor shows a good detection sensitivity toward formaldehyde (2.9 mg L−1) vapors when the QCM sensor operates at different humidity conditions up to 75 %RH (Fig. 10).

The amine vapor uptakes into CQDs film may be described by a pseudo-first-order mass transfer between the vapor phase and the adsorption sites of CQDs. The CQDs-coated QCM sensor (m' = 74.108 μg) was exposed to formaldehyde vapors and other VOCs, including aniline, pyrrole, pyridine, xylene, benzene, and toluene with the same concentration (10 mg L−1) and the time changes of Δf were recorded as shown in Fig. 11 (a). The pseudo-first-order rate constant (k) can be considered as a basis of comparing the rates of vapor uptake following the equation (Chagger et al., 1995):
(3)
Δ f
t
Δ f
∞
= 1 −
e
− k t
where, Δf t is the frequency change due to adsorption at time (t) while Δf ∞ refers to the frequency change at equilibrium state at the end of the adsorption process. The two former frequency change parameters can be calculated from the following relations:
(4) Δf t = f CQDs– f t and Δf ∞ = f CQDs– f ∞
where, f t is the frequency during the exposure process at time t. Δf t and Δf ∞ refer to the adsorbate weight uptake at time (t) and at equilibrium, respectively, as previously defined. A graph of ln (1- Δf t/Δf ∞) against t gives a linear regression of gradient k. The values of k were estimated from the gradient of the plot of ln(1-Δf t/Δf ∞) against t (Fig. 11(b)) and are listed in Table 4. A rapid initial uptake of formaldehyde molecules followed by linear regression was obtained as seen in Fig. 11(b). The determined k values for all analytes' vapors are summarized in Table 5 . Accordingly, these results demonstrated that the rate of formaldehyde vapor uptake by the CQDs-modified QCM proceeds faster than other aromatic VOCs which are approximately 1.34, 1.65, 1,72, 1.33, 1.31, 1.49 times greater rate than aniline, pyrrole, pyridine, xylene, benzene, toluene, respectively, probably due to the low vapor pressure (1.207 at 25 °C) in addition to the small chemical volume and structure of formaldehyde (Handa and Jones, 1975). For a selective detection of formaldehyde vapor among the other tested chemical-vapor analytes, a combination of dynamic (kinetic) and static (Δf) values can be considered as a basis of discrimination.

An ecofriendly approach was demonstrated to prepare CQDs with about 4 nm average size by hydrothermal treatment of laurel leaves. A sensor of as-prepared CQDs and QCM electrode was fabricated to detect formaldehyde vapors in air at room temperature. The fabricated sensor is rapid and showed a distinguished sensitivity and selectivity for formaldehyde with good reproducibility, and reversibility over other VOCs, including aniline, pyridine, and pyrrole vapors. The adsorption kinetics of aniline, pyridine, and pyrrole vapors were also evaluated. The dynamic (kinetic) and static (frequency changes) obtained values can be viewed as a basis for the selective detection of formaldehyde vapor among the other tested analytes. The humidity was found to possess a limited effect on the sensing response of the QCM-based sensor.
